message:
fix(core): Resolve inference bugs and compilation warnings

- fix(bocpd): Correct Normal-Gamma update logic to use incremental formula.
- fix(hawkes): Fix recursive intensity calculation in EM algorithm.
- fix(renice): Remove unnecessary mutable variable binding.
- fix(freeze): Remove unused import.
- fix(supervision): Export read_environ for tests.
- feat(inference): Add IMM filter implementation.

files.patch:
@@ -331,17 +331,17 @@
 {"id":"process_triage-nao.13","title":"Implement hidden semi-Markov state model feature extractor (Gamma durations)","description":"## Overview
Implement Plan §2(F)/§4.5’s **hidden semi-Markov chain** framing as a feature extractor:
- discrete hidden state `S_t ∈ {useful, useful-bad, abandoned, zombie}`
- state durations `D_S ~ Gamma(α_{D,S}, β_{D,S})`

This does *not* replace the conjugate Naive Bayes core; it produces duration/regime summary features (e.g., hazard inflation indicators) that the core can consume.

## Requirements
- Define a minimal HSMM representation for regime labels over a window.
- Use Gamma duration priors consistent with the plan parameterization (shape/rate).
- Emit deterministic summaries:
  - most likely regime sequence (or top-k)
  - per-regime duration posteriors or hazard indicators
  - “regime switch” evidence suitable for ledger display

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] HSMM outputs are deterministic for fixed windowed inputs.
- [ ] Duration modeling uses Gamma shape/rate consistently.
- [ ] Outputs can be disabled; default path remains simpler inference.

## Test Plan
- Unit: synthetic sequences with known regime shifts and durations.
- Regression: ensure stable summaries across runs.
- Integration: ensure summaries can be attached to `proc_features`.

 {"id":"process_triage-nao.14","title":"Implement Markov-modulated compound Poisson (Levy subordinator) CPU burst feature layer","description":"## Overview
Implement Plan §4.6 / §2(G): a **Markov-modulated compound Poisson** CPU burst model as a feature extractor.

Model (from plan):
- `N(t) ~ Poisson(κ_S * t)`
- burst sizes `X_i ~ Exp(β_S)`
- cumulative CPU burst mass: `C(t) = Σ_{i=1..N(t)} X_i`

This yields a compound Poisson (finite-activity Lévy subordinator) with closed-form Laplace transform. We treat inference here as a feature layer (moment matching / EM / augmentation), emitting deterministic summaries to the closed-form decision core.

## Requirements
- Define what “events” constitute CPU bursts for this layer (e.g., busy runs, tick bursts, scheduler latency bursts).
- Fit parameters per regime/state S (or per cmd category) offline or per-window:
  - κ_S (event rate)
  - β_S (burst size scale)
- Emit deterministic summary features:
  - estimated burst rate and severity
  - regime-conditioned burstiness indicator
  - an explainable contribution term for the evidence ledger

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Outputs are deterministic for a fixed event stream/window.
- [ ] Layer can be disabled; default core remains unchanged.
- [ ] Summaries are explainable (rate + burst size, not opaque embeddings).

## Test Plan
- Unit: synthetic burst event sequences with known κ/β.
- Integration: ensure summaries attach to `proc_features` without excessive overhead.

 {"id":"process_triage-nao.15","title":"Implement process genealogy priors (Galton-Watson) + orphan Bayes factor evidence","description":"## Overview
Implement Plan §4.12 / §2(P): explicit **process genealogy** modeling features:
- PPID tree as a Bayesian network
- Galton–Watson branching model for expected child activity
- Orphan/reparenting evidence as a Bayes factor term
- “cobweb” causality representation for explainability

This complements (does not replace) belief propagation on PPID trees: it provides interpretable genealogy-derived priors/evidence contributions.

## Key formula (from plan)
Orphan Bayes factor:
- `BF_orphan = P(unexpected_reparenting | abandoned) / P(unexpected_reparenting | useful)`

Crucial hygiene:
- PPID=1 alone is weak evidence; condition on supervision/session context.

## Requirements
- Define genealogy feature extraction:
  - ancestry chain summary
  - child-count/activity expectations under a branching prior
  - orphan/reparenting BF contribution term (with conditioning)
- Emit explainability-friendly artifacts:
  - a “genealogy card” that can be shown in TUI/agent explain.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Genealogy evidence term is computed and logged with conditioning rationale.
- [ ] Branching prior outputs are deterministic for fixed PPID graphs.
- [ ] Outputs are explainable and can be surfaced in the ledger.

## Test Plan
- Unit: fixture PPID trees (including supervision and true orphans) and verify BF contribution logic.
- Integration: ensure genealogy features integrate with existing PPID BP layer without double counting.

-{"id":"process_triage-nao.16","title":"Implement belief-state update utilities (POMDP approximation)","description":"## Overview
Implement Plan §4.14 / §2(U)/§2(AH): the belief-state update used for myopic POMDP-style sequential decisions.

This provides the **belief update math**; decision policy selection lives in Phase 5.

## Model (from plan)
Belief update:
- `b_{t+1}(S) ∝ P(x_{t+1} | S) · Σ_{S'} P(S | S') · b_t(S')`

## Requirements
- Define a discrete transition model `P(S|S')` over the 4-state space.
- Provide a belief update function that consumes:
  - prior belief `b_t`
  - new evidence likelihood terms `P(x_{t+1}|S)` from the closed-form core
- Emit intermediate quantities for explainability (transition contribution vs evidence contribution).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Belief update is implemented for the 4-state space.
- [ ] Outputs are numerically stable and normalized.
- [ ] Intermediate contributions can be surfaced in galaxy-brain.

## Test Plan
- Unit: small synthetic transition matrices and evidence likelihoods with hand-checkable updates.
- Regression: ensure normalization and stability across edge cases.

 {"id":"process_triage-nao.17","title":"Implement Beta-Stacy discrete-time survival model (Bayesian nonparametric hazard)","description":"## Overview
Implement Plan §4.21 / §2(AC): **Bayesian nonparametric survival** via a discrete-time hazard model with Beta priors (Beta–Stacy style).

This provides a flexible, closed-form hazard model for long-tail “stuckness” that can be more robust than simple Gamma lifetime assumptions.

## Model (from plan)
Bin time into bins t=1..T with at-risk count n_t and event count d_t:
- Prior: `h_t ~ Beta(a_t, b_t)`
- Posterior: `h_t | data ~ Beta(a_t + d_t, b_t + n_t - d_t)`
- Survival: `S(t) = ∏_{j=1..t} (1 - h_j)`

## Requirements
- Define a binning scheme (time bins + provenance).
- Implement conjugate updates per bin.
- Emit summary outputs usable by:
  - runtime evidence terms
  - trajectory prediction/time-to-threshold features
  - galaxy-brain hazard cards (showing a_t/b_t updates)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Posterior updates are closed-form and stable.
- [ ] Survival curve can be computed from posteriors.
- [ ] Binning scheme is versioned and auditable.

## Test Plan
- Unit: synthetic lifetime datasets with known bin counts; verify posterior parameters.
- Regression: stable outputs across runs.
- Integration: ensure survival summaries can be attached to inference outputs without double-counting runtime evidence.

-{"id":"process_triage-nao.18","title":"Extend Hawkes layer to multivariate cross-excitation summaries","description":"## Overview
Implement Plan §2(AI): extend the Hawkes point-process layer to **multivariate Hawkes** (cross-excitation across metrics):
- syscalls → IO bursts → network bursts, etc.

This remains a feature extractor: it emits deterministic cross-excitation summaries that feed the closed-form decision core.

## Requirements
- Define a small set of event streams/marks to model jointly (CPU busy, IO bytes, net bytes, syscall rate).
- Fit exponential-kernel multivariate Hawkes parameters (best-effort / bounded) and emit:
  - self-excitation strengths
  - cross-excitation strengths
  - intensity estimates over window
- Ensure overhead is bounded; allow disabling.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Cross-excitation features are emitted deterministically for fixed input streams.
- [ ] Can be disabled and is gated by instrumentation availability.

## Test Plan
- Unit: synthetic multi-stream sequences where cross-excitation is known and verify parameter recovery qualitatively.
- Integration: ensure outputs attach to `proc_features` and ledger.

-{"id":"process_triage-nao.19","title":"Implement Sinkhorn divergence approximation for drift detection","description":"## Overview
Implement Plan §2(BN): a practical, fast approximation to Wasserstein drift detection via **Sinkhorn divergence**.

This complements (does not replace) exact 1D Wasserstein computations. It is used when:
- distributions are higher-dimensional, or
- streaming responsiveness matters.

## Requirements
- Implement Sinkhorn divergence (or a conservative proxy) for selected feature distributions.
- Expose a drift score and integrate into existing drift/PPC/DRO gates.
- Ensure overhead is bounded; allow disabling.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Drift scores are deterministic for fixed inputs.
- [ ] Integrates with drift gating without changing the closed-form posterior.

## Test Plan
- Unit: small synthetic distributions with known divergence ordering.
- Regression: stable outputs across runs.
- Performance: confirm bounded runtime for typical sample sizes.

-{"id":"process_triage-nao.2","title":"Implement wavelet/spectral periodicity features for behavioral signals","description":"## Context
Phase 4. Periodicity features help distinguish “healthy periodic activity” from “stuck spin” and detect recurring bursts.

## Approach
- Inputs: time-series samples from shadow-mode storage (CPU, IO, net).
- Compute periodicity summaries:
  - dominant frequency bands
  - burst periodicity score
  - stability of periodic components over time
- Feed summaries into inference as additional features with documented likelihood models.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Detects periodic vs non-periodic synthetic series with high precision.
- [ ] Computation is bounded and feature-gated (won’t blow up on large fleets).
- [ ] Outputs are auditable (include sample window, method, summary statistics).

## Test Plan
- Unit tests: FFT/wavelet utilities and normalization.
- Golden tests: synthetic periodic signals (sinusoid, square wave, Poisson bursts).

 {"id":"process_triage-nao.20","title":"Implement minimax / least-favorable prior gating (robust Bayes extension)","description":"## Overview
Implement Plan §4.17 / §2(X): **minimax / least-favorable prior** extensions to robust Bayes.

Purpose: act only when the recommendation is robust under adversarial/noisy conditions.

## Requirements
- Define a credal set / family of priors (consistent with robust Bayes bead).
- Compute a conservative (worst-case) expected loss across the set.
- Expose explainability:
  - show whether the decision is stable across priors and how much the threshold shifts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Worst-case expected-loss gating is implemented and auditable.
- [ ] Defaults are conservative and can be disabled.

## Test Plan
- Unit: small synthetic examples where the least-favorable prior flips the decision.
- Regression: default settings preserve baseline behavior.

-{"id":"process_triage-nao.21","title":"Implement renewal-reward / semi-regenerative process summaries for CPU/IO","description":"## Overview
Implement Plan §4.27 / §2(AR): **renewal reward** / semi-regenerative process summaries as deterministic features.

Idea: model rewards (CPU/IO work) accrued between renewal events (e.g., bursts, loop iterations) and estimate reward rates with conjugate updates.

## Requirements
- Define renewal event proxies (e.g., IO burst boundaries, scheduler-state transitions, periodic workload cycles).
- Compute per-PID renewal-reward summaries:
  - reward rate estimates
  - variability measures
  - evidence terms indicating “productive progress” vs “wasted spinning”
- Ensure summaries are explainable and bounded.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Deterministic summary outputs for fixed input sequences.
- [ ] Can be disabled; default pipeline unaffected.

## Test Plan
- Unit: synthetic renewal sequences with known reward rates.
- Integration: attach summaries into `proc_features` and verify ledger surfacing.

+{"id":"process_triage-nao.21","title":"Implement renewal-reward / semi-regenerative process summaries for CPU/IO","description":"## Overview
Implement Plan §4.27 / §2(AR): **renewal reward** / semi-regenerative process summaries as deterministic features.

Idea: model rewards (CPU/IO work) accrued between renewal events (e.g., bursts, loop iterations) and estimate reward rates with conjugate updates.

## Requirements
- Define renewal event proxies (e.g., IO burst boundaries, scheduler-state transitions, periodic workload cycles).
- Compute per-PID renewal-reward summaries:
  - reward rate estimates
  - variability measures
  - evidence terms indicating “productive progress” vs “wasted spinning”
- Ensure summaries are explainable and bounded.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Deterministic summary outputs for fixed input sequences.
- [ ] Can be disabled; default pipeline unaffected.

## Test Plan
- Unit: synthetic renewal sequences with known reward rates.
- Integration: attach summaries into `proc_features` and verify ledger surfacing.

 {"id":"process_triage-nao.3","title":"Implement EVT tail modeling (POT/GPD) for extreme spikes","description":"## Context
Phase 4. Extreme value behavior (rare spikes) can indicate pathological states or risk. The plan calls for EVT tail modeling as an advanced feature extractor.

## Approach
- Inputs: time-series of metrics with rare spikes (CPU, memory growth jumps, IO bursts).
- Fit Peaks-Over-Threshold (POT) with Generalized Pareto Distribution (GPD) on exceedances.
- Emit summaries:
  - tail index estimate
  - exceedance rate
  - tail risk score used by decision theory (e.g., CVaR gating)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] On synthetic EVT fixtures, tail index estimates are within tolerance.
- [ ] Produces conservative outputs when data is insufficient.
- [ ] Integrates as optional feature; default runs unaffected.

## Test Plan
- Golden tests: synthetic GPD samples.
- Property tests: heavier tail → higher tail risk score.

 {"id":"process_triage-nao.4","title":"Implement distribution drift detection (Wasserstein + gates)","description":"## Context
Phase 4. Drift detection supports robustness: when the world changes, the model should become conservative.

## Problem
Model assumptions can drift across machines/time (new toolchains, OS updates, new workloads). We need to detect drift relative to baseline and gate aggressive actions.

## Approach
- Compute distribution distances between current feature distributions and historical baselines.
- Use Wasserstein distance (and/or other stable distances) as a drift metric.
- Emit drift flags that:
  - down-weight inference confidence
  - tighten policy gates (DRO / conservative thresholds)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Drift flags trigger on synthetic drift fixtures and remain quiet on stationary fixtures.
- [ ] Drift gating integrates with decision theory (e.g., increases loss for kill).
- [ ] Drift computation is bounded and incremental.

## Test Plan
- Golden tests: stationary vs shifted distributions.
- Performance tests: drift computation on large process sets.

 {"id":"process_triage-nao.5","title":"Implement streaming sketches/heavy-hitter summaries for high-rate events","description":"## Context
Phase 4. The plan calls for sketches/heavy-hitters to summarize high-rate event streams efficiently.

## Problem
In daemon/fleet modes, event streams can be high volume. Persisting everything is expensive; we need approximate summaries (top offenders, quantiles) with bounded memory.

## Approach
- Implement streaming summaries:
  - heavy hitters for command/signature patterns
  - quantile sketches for resource metrics
- Store summaries in telemetry as compact records.
- Use summaries for:
  - quick UX overviews (“top 10 offenders”)
  - baseline fitting acceleration

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Memory usage is bounded independent of stream length.
- [ ] Accuracy meets documented bounds on synthetic streams.
- [ ] Summaries integrate into reporting and/or inference as optional features.

## Test Plan
- Unit tests: sketch correctness on small streams.
- Simulation tests: heavy hitter recovery vs exact counts.

-{"id":"process_triage-nao.6","title":"Implement switching state-space models (IMM) as feature extractors","description":"## Context
Phase 4. The plan calls for regime-switching dynamics modeling (IMM / switching LDS) to capture transitions between behavioral regimes.

## Approach
- Treat as an optional feature extractor:
  - estimate regime probabilities over time (e.g., idle vs active vs stuck)
  - emit regime-change indicators and smoothed state estimates
- Integrate outputs as evidence features and/or as priors for hazard/regime models.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Produces sensible regime probabilities on synthetic regime-switching series.
- [ ] Computation is bounded and can be disabled.
- [ ] Outputs are auditable (parameters + diagnostics).

## Test Plan
- Golden tests: synthetic switching series.
- Regression tests: ensure stability under noise.

 {"id":"process_triage-nao.7","title":"Implement Bayesian model averaging over inference submodels","description":"## Context
Phase 4. Combine multiple inference submodels without committing to a single brittle model.

## Problem
Different models may perform better in different contexts (dev server vs test runner vs daemon). Model averaging provides robustness and improves calibration.

## Approach
- Maintain a set of inference submodels (feature subsets / likelihood variants).
- Compute per-submodel posteriors and combine via weighted average.
- Weights can be:
  - fixed priors (initial)
  - updated via shadow-mode performance (later)
- Ensure explainability:
  - ledger must show per-submodel contributions and final mixture.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Produces combined posterior that matches weighted mixture definition.
- [ ] Weights are explicit and auditable.
- [ ] Galaxy-brain output can attribute results to submodels.

## Test Plan
- Unit tests: mixture math and normalization.
- Golden tests: small fixture where submodels disagree.

 {"id":"process_triage-nao.8","title":"Implement robust statistics summaries for noisy evidence","description":"## Context
Phase 4. Robust summaries reduce sensitivity to noisy or adversarial measurements.

## Scope
- Robust estimators for:
  - central tendency (median, trimmed mean)
  - dispersion (MAD)
  - trend (Theil–Sen / Huber)
- Use robust summaries for:
  - smoothing CPU/mem series
  - outlier handling in likelihood inputs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Robust summaries behave correctly on fixtures with injected outliers.
- [ ] Integrated summaries are used in at least one inference feature path.

## Test Plan
- Unit tests: robust estimator correctness.
- Property tests: bounded influence (single outlier doesn’t dominate).

 {"id":"process_triage-nao.9","title":"Implement graph-based smoothing priors beyond PPID trees (Laplacian)","description":"## Context
Phase 4. Extends coupled inference beyond tree-only PPID structure.

## Problem
PPID gives a forest, but dependencies can also arise from:
- shared cgroups
- shared working directory/project
- shared ports
These create non-tree graphs. Exact BP is no longer tractable; we need conservative approximations.

## Approach
- Build an adjacency graph from selected relations (cgroup, cwd cluster, port-sharing).
- Apply graph Laplacian smoothing (or other conservative coupling) to produce adjusted priors/feature summaries.
- Keep closed-form core intact: smoothing yields deterministic prior adjustments.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Produces stable outputs on small fixture graphs.
- [ ] Can be disabled; default remains tree-only coupling.

## Test Plan
- Unit tests: graph construction from fixtures.
- Golden tests: smoothing results on known graphs.

@@ -408,15 +417,21 @@ fn loss_for_action(
         Action::Throttle => row
             .throttle
             .ok_or(DecisionError::MissingLoss { action, class }),
+        // Quarantine uses Throttle's loss value (semantically similar: resource restriction)
+        Action::Quarantine => row
+            .throttle
+            .ok_or(DecisionError::MissingLoss { action, class }),
         Action::Renice => row
             .renice
             .ok_or(DecisionError::MissingLoss { action, class }),

+        // Resume/Unfreeze/Unquarantine are follow-up actions, not primary decisions, so no loss entry
+        Action::Resume | Action::Unfreeze | Action::Unquarantine => {
+            Err(DecisionError::MissingLoss { action, class })
+        }
     }
 }
